section.title: Righteousness Through Faith
section.sectionRationale: This is the mountain peak of the letter and of the gospel itself. After proving the whole world guilty, Paul turns with two of the sweetest words in Scripture: but now. A righteousness of God apart from the law has been revealed, witnessed by the Law and the Prophets, given through faith in Jesus Christ to all who believe. Guilty sinners are justified freely by His grace through the redemption in Christ Jesus, whom God set forth as a propitiation in His blood. In the cross God shows Himself to be both just and the justifier of the one who has faith in Jesus. Boasting is excluded, and the God of Jew and Gentile alike justifies by faith.
section.thesis: Because no one can be justified by works of the law, God has provided His own righteousness as a free gift, received by faith in Jesus Christ, grounded in the redemption and propitiating death of Christ, so that God is shown to be both perfectly just and the justifier of every believing sinner, Jew or Gentile, and all human boasting is forever excluded.
section.frameNote: Three movements: but now the righteousness of God revealed, given by faith to all who believe and justifying freely by grace; the cross as the propitiation that shows God to be just and the justifier; and the exclusion of boasting, with the one God justifying Jew and Gentile by faith while upholding the law.

units.label: But now the righteousness of God revealed
units.note: justified freely by grace, received by faith

verses.text: But now, apart from the law, the {{dikaiosyne|righteousness}} of God has been revealed, as attested by the Law and the Prophets.
And this righteousness from God comes through {{pistis|faith}} in Jesus Christ to all who believe. There is no distinction,
for all have sinned and fall short of the glory of God,
and are justified freely by His {{charis|grace}} through the {{apolytrosis|redemption}} that is in Christ Jesus.

units.label: God set Him forth as a propitiation
units.note: the cross shows God just and the justifier

verses.text: God presented Him as an {{hilasterion|atoning sacrifice}} in His blood through faith, in order to demonstrate His righteousness, because in His forbearance He had passed over the sins committed beforehand.
He did this to demonstrate His righteousness at the present time, so as to be just and to justify the one who has faith in Jesus.

units.label: Boasting excluded, God of Jew and Gentile alike
units.note: justified by faith, yet the law upheld

verses.text: Where, then, is boasting? It is excluded. On what principle? On that of works? No, but on that of faith.
For we maintain that a man is justified by faith apart from works of the law.
Is God the God of Jews only? Is He not the God of Gentiles too? Yes, of Gentiles too,
since there is only one God, who will justify the circumcised by faith and the uncircumcised through that same faith.
Do we, then, nullify the law by this faith? Certainly not! Instead, we uphold the law.

dikaiosyne.gloss: Righteousness, a right standing before God and the saving activity of God that provides it. This righteousness is of God, from Him as gift and source, revealed apart from the law and yet witnessed by it. It is not a righteousness we produce but one God grants, credited to the believer through faith in Christ, the heart of the doctrine of justification.

pistis.gloss: Faith, trust, the empty hand that receives what God gives. Righteousness comes through faith in Jesus Christ to all who believe. Faith is not a work that earns; it is the means by which the sinner rests wholly on Christ and receives God's gift. Paul repeats it to exclude all boasting: the ground is Christ, the instrument is faith.

charis.gloss: Grace, God's unearned favor toward the undeserving. Sinners are justified freely by His grace, a gift with no price attached and no merit required. Grace is the fountain of justification; because salvation is by grace it can only be received by faith, and boasting is forever shut out.

apolytrosis.gloss: Redemption, release secured by the payment of a ransom, as of a slave set free. Justification is free to us but costly to God, grounded in the redemption that is in Christ Jesus. The price was His own blood; the gift comes to us without charge because the ransom has already been paid in full.

hilasterion.gloss: Propitiation, the atoning sacrifice that turns away wrath, the word used for the mercy seat where blood was sprinkled on the Day of Atonement. God set forth Christ as the true mercy seat, bearing in His blood the wrath our sins deserved, so that God can be both just, not overlooking sin, and the justifier of all who trust in Jesus.

basic.context: Chapters 1 to 3 have driven relentlessly to one verdict: the whole world is guilty and no one can be justified by works of the law. Now, at the darkest point, Paul turns with but now. Everything changes. God Himself has provided the righteousness that no sinner could achieve, revealed in the gospel and grounded in the death of Jesus. This is the answer the letter has been building toward.
basic.hermeneutics: Read these verses slowly; every word is loaded. Righteousness of God, faith, grace, redemption, propitiation, justify, blood. Verses 21 to 26 form one of the densest and most important paragraphs in the Bible, often called the heart of Romans. The but now signals a decisive turn in the history of salvation: the promised righteousness has appeared. Verses 27 to 31 draw out the consequences, especially that boasting is excluded and the gospel is for Jew and Gentile alike.
basic.originalLanguages: The dikaiosyne, the righteousness of God, comes through pistis, faith, and is given by charis, grace. It rests on the apolytrosis, the redemption, the ransom paid in Christ, and on the hilasterion, the propitiation, Christ set forth as the atoning sacrifice in His blood. These words together carry the doctrine of justification: a gift, received by faith, purchased at the cross.
basic.typology: The hilasterion is the mercy seat of the ark, where atoning blood was sprinkled once a year; Christ is the true mercy seat, the place where God and sinners meet in mercy. The redemption echoes the exodus, God buying a people out of slavery, now fulfilled as Christ ransoms us from sin. The Law and the Prophets bear witness to this very righteousness.
basic.christ: Christ is everything here. He is the object of saving faith, the one in whom redemption is found, and the propitiation God set forth in His blood. At the cross He bore the wrath our sins deserved, so that God could remain just while justifying the guilty. The whole gospel narrows to a single scene: Jesus, the crucified mercy seat, in whom God is both the just Judge and the justifier of everyone who believes.
basic.god: God is the source, the actor, and the vindicated one in this passage. He reveals His righteousness, He justifies freely by His grace, He sets forth His Son as the propitiation, and He does it to demonstrate His own justice. The cross is not only love for sinners but the vindication of God's holiness: He does not sweep sin under the rug but deals with it fully, so as to be both just and the justifier.
basic.crossReferences: Leviticus 16 describes the mercy seat and the Day of Atonement behind the word propitiation. Habakkuk 2:4, the righteous will live by faith, is the letter's thesis. Ephesians 2:8-9 restates that we are saved by grace through faith, not by works, so no one may boast. Genesis 15:6, Abraham believed God and it was credited as righteousness, grounds justification by faith in the very Law that testifies to it.
basic.oneStory: The whole Bible had promised that God would put things right and provide a righteousness His people could not earn. Sacrifices, the mercy seat, and the exodus all pointed forward. Here the promise arrives: but now the righteousness of God is revealed in Christ crucified, the true propitiation and redemption. The long story of a holy God saving guilty sinners without compromising His justice reaches its climax at the cross, thrown open to Jew and Gentile alike.

context.literary: Romans 3:21-31 is the turning point of the letter, answering the universal guilt established in 1:18-3:20. Verses 21 to 26 are a single, tightly packed exposition of justification, and verses 27 to 31 apply it by excluding boasting and uniting Jew and Gentile. The emphatic but now of verse 21 marks the pivot from wrath revealed to righteousness revealed.
context.historical: Paul writes around AD 57 to a mixed church in Rome. The question of how Jew and Gentile relate in one people of God was pressing. Paul answers it at the deepest level: since all have sinned and all are justified the same way, by faith apart from works of the law, there is one God and one gospel for both, and no ground for ethnic or ceremonial pride.
context.canonical: This paragraph is the classic biblical statement of justification by grace through faith, the doctrine the Reformers called the article on which the church stands or falls. It gathers the sacrificial system, the mercy seat, and the promise to Abraham into the cross of Christ, and it grounds the great Reformation confessions of grace alone, faith alone, and Christ alone.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: Paul intends to announce the good news his whole argument has prepared for: that God has provided in Christ the righteousness no sinner could earn, received by faith apart from works, so that guilty people are justly forgiven, God's own justice is upheld, all boasting is excluded, and Jew and Gentile are joined in one gospel.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The passage describes the decisive act of God in revealing and providing righteousness in Christ. It prescribes the abiding way of salvation for every person in every age: justification is by grace through faith in Jesus, apart from works of the law, and it calls all who read to rest their whole hope on the crucified Savior.

debates.question: Does through faith in Jesus Christ mean the believer's faith in Christ or the faithfulness of Christ himself?

views.label: The believer's faith in Christ
views.case: The historic and majority reading takes the phrase as objective: righteousness comes through our faith directed at Christ. The immediate context, to all who believe and the one who has faith in Jesus, strongly supports faith as the sinner's trust in Christ.

views.label: The faithfulness of Christ
views.case: Some recent scholars render it the faithfulness of Christ, His faithful obedience unto death as the ground of salvation, with the believer's faith mentioned separately in the same verse.

views.label: Both held together
views.case: Others hold that Christ's faithfulness is the ground and our faith the instrument, so the two need not be opposed; the gift rests on His finished work and is received by our trust.

debates.bearing: The traditional reading safeguards that we are justified by faith, not by our own works, and the alternatives, rightly framed, still ground salvation wholly in Christ. Either way the sinner contributes no merit and receives everything by grace.

debates.question: What does it mean that God set forth Christ as a propitiation (hilasterion)?

views.label: Propitiation, the turning away of wrath
views.case: The historic view, recovered against attempts to soften it, holds that Christ bore the wrath our sins deserved, satisfying God's just judgment so that mercy and justice meet. This alone explains how God can be just and the justifier.

views.label: Expiation, the cleansing of sin
views.case: Some prefer expiation, the wiping away of sin, arguing the term stresses the removal of guilt rather than the appeasing of anger, though most acknowledge wrath is in view given 1:18.

views.label: The mercy seat
views.case: Others emphasize the cultic background, that hilasterion names the mercy seat of the ark, so Christ is the place of atonement where God meets sinners in mercy through blood.

debates.bearing: The propitiation reading protects the seriousness of God's justice and the costliness of the cross; without wrath borne, the demonstration of God's righteousness in verses 25 and 26 loses its force. Christ as both mercy seat and wrath-bearer secures a real, just salvation.

study.originalLanguages: This paragraph carries the vocabulary of the gospel. The dikaiosyne, the righteousness of God, is revealed apart from the law and given through pistis, faith, to all who believe. Sinners are justified by charis, grace, as a free gift, resting on the apolytrosis, the redemption, the ransom paid in Christ Jesus. At the center stands the hilasterion, the propitiation: God set forth His Son as the atoning sacrifice in His blood, the true mercy seat where wrath is turned away. Together these words declare that righteousness is God's gift, received by faith, grounded in the blood of the cross.

typology.fulfillment: The hilasterion, the cover of the ark where atoning blood was sprinkled on the Day of Atonement, is fulfilled in Christ, the true meeting place of God and sinners through His blood, Leviticus 16.
God's buying of a people out of slavery in the exodus is fulfilled in the apolytrosis in Christ, who ransoms us from bondage to sin by His death.
The promise that Abraham believed God and it was counted to him as righteousness is fulfilled as God justifies all who believe, Jew and Gentile, by faith, Genesis 15:6.

study.christ: Everything in this passage converges on Christ crucified. He is the one in whom redemption is found, the propitiation God publicly set forth, the atoning sacrifice in His own blood. On the cross He absorbed the wrath our sins deserved, so that God could be both perfectly just and the justifier of the guilty who trust in Him. He is the true mercy seat, the place where holiness and mercy meet, and He is the object of the faith that saves. The Reformers rightly saw here the very heart of the gospel: Christ alone, His finished work the sole ground of our acceptance, received by faith alone, to the glory of grace alone.
study.god: This is one of Scripture's clearest windows into the character of God. He is holy and just, unwilling to overlook sin, and He is gracious and merciful, unwilling to leave sinners to perish. At the cross both are perfectly displayed: God demonstrates His righteousness by dealing fully with sin in the death of His Son, and He justifies freely the one who has faith in Jesus. He is the one God of Jew and Gentile alike, who saves both the same way, by faith, so that all the glory belongs to His grace and none to human achievement.

crossReferences.note: The blood sprinkled on the mercy seat on the Day of Atonement, the background to Christ as the propitiation.
The righteous will live by faith, the thesis of Romans, fulfilled in justification through faith in Christ.
Abraham believed God and it was credited to him as righteousness, the pattern of justification by faith the Law itself attests.
By grace you have been saved through faith, not by works, so that no one can boast, echoing the exclusion of boasting here.

study.oneStory: From the first sacrifice in Eden, through the mercy seat and the exodus and the promise to Abraham, the Bible pointed forward to a righteousness God would provide and a redemption He would accomplish. At the darkest moment of the human story, when every mouth is stopped and the whole world stands guilty, God speaks His but now. In Christ crucified, the true mercy seat and ransom, He reveals His saving righteousness, upholds His justice, and justifies freely all who believe, Jew and Gentile together, gathering one people to the praise of His grace.

quiz.q: What decisive turn does Paul announce with the words but now in verse 21?

options.text: A righteousness of God, apart from the law, has been revealed and attested by the Law and the Prophets.
options.note: Verse 21 exactly: the righteousness of God is now revealed apart from the law, witnessed by Scripture.

options.text: God has unveiled His own saving righteousness quite apart from law keeping.
options.note: Right in substance, though it omits that the Law and the Prophets bear witness to it.

options.text: God has finally made the law easier for people to keep.
options.note: The righteousness comes apart from the law, not by making law keeping easier.

options.text: God has abandoned righteousness as a requirement altogether.
options.note: The opposite; God reveals and upholds His righteousness while giving it as a gift.

quiz.q: On what basis are guilty sinners justified, according to verse 24?

options.text: Freely by His grace, through the redemption that is in Christ Jesus.
options.note: Verse 24 states it word for word: justified freely by grace through the redemption in Christ.

options.text: As a free gift of God's favor, grounded in Christ's redeeming work.
options.note: Accurate in meaning; the text names grace as the source and redemption in Christ as the ground.

options.text: By a combination of God's grace and their own sincere efforts.
options.note: Paul says freely by grace; adding human effort contradicts the gift and reintroduces boasting.

options.text: By strictly keeping the works of the law.
options.note: Paul has just said no one is justified by works of the law; justification is by grace.

quiz.q: What does it mean that God set Christ forth as a propitiation in His blood (verses 25 to 26)?

options.text: Christ bore in His blood the wrath our sins deserved, so God is both just and the justifier.
options.note: The propitiation turns away wrath, letting God remain just while justifying believers.

options.text: At the cross God dealt fully with sin so He could forgive without compromising His justice.
options.note: Correct in substance; the fuller answer names the wrath-bearing propitiation in Christ's blood.

options.text: God simply overlooked sin out of kindness without any atoning sacrifice.
options.note: The passage says the opposite; God demonstrates His righteousness rather than overlooking sin.

options.text: Christ set a moral example that inspires people to reform themselves.
options.note: The cross is a propitiation in His blood, not merely an inspiring example.

quiz.q: Why is human boasting excluded (verses 27 to 28)?

options.text: Because a person is justified by faith apart from works of the law, so no one contributes merit.
options.note: Verses 27 and 28: boasting is excluded on the principle of faith, not works.

options.text: Because salvation is received by faith, not achieved by our own doing.
options.note: Right in meaning; Paul specifies justification by faith apart from works of the law.

options.text: Because only a few especially holy people are allowed to boast.
options.note: Paul excludes all boasting, not merely most of it; the ground is faith, not attainment.

options.text: Because good works count for nothing in the Christian life ever.
options.note: Paul excludes boasting in works as the ground of justification, not the value of good works as fruit.

quiz.q: How does Paul answer whether justification by faith nullifies the law (verse 31)?

options.text: Certainly not; instead, we uphold the law.
options.note: Verse 31 exactly: faith does not nullify but upholds the law.

options.text: No; this faith actually establishes and confirms the law rather than cancelling it.
options.note: Correct in meaning; Paul says we uphold the law, not overthrow it.

options.text: Yes; faith makes the law completely irrelevant and void.
options.note: Paul says certainly not; faith upholds the law rather than voiding it.

options.text: The question does not matter, since the law was never from God.
options.note: Paul honors the law as God's; he affirms that faith upholds it.

questions: Paul turns the whole argument with but now. How does it strike you that the announcement of grace comes precisely at the point where every mouth is stopped and no one can save himself?
Righteousness comes freely by grace through faith, not by works. Where are you still tempted to add your own performance to Christ's finished work as the ground of your acceptance?
At the cross God is shown to be both just and the justifier. Why does it matter for your assurance that God did not merely overlook your sin but dealt with it fully in Christ?
The word propitiation means Christ bore the wrath your sin deserved. How does dwelling on the cost of your redemption deepen your love and worship?
Boasting is excluded, and God justifies Jew and Gentile the same way. How should being saved entirely by grace shape your humility toward God and your welcome of others who trust in Christ?
Paul insists that faith upholds rather than nullifies the law. How does being justified by faith free you to love God's commands rather than fear them?

translationNotes.note: Through faith in Jesus Christ can be read as the believer's faith in Christ, the historic and contextual reading, or as the faithfulness of Christ. The surrounding phrases, to all who believe and the one who has faith in Jesus, favor faith as the sinner's trust; either way salvation rests wholly on Christ.

The word rendered atoning sacrifice is hilasterion, meaning propitiation, the turning away of wrath, and also the term for the mercy seat of the ark. The BSB footnote itself offers or as a propitiation. Christ is both the wrath-bearer and the true mercy seat where God meets sinners in mercy.

Justified by faith apart from works of the law is the sentence at the heart of the Reformation. Works of the law are deeds of obedience to the Mosaic law; Paul denies they justify, insisting acceptance with God is by faith alone in Christ.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Romans 3:21-31. Footnote markers were omitted from the running text per format. No em or en dashes are present in this passage's BSB text. Units 1 and 2 marked as climax, as this paragraph is the theological peak of the letter. Sermon left as none; no expositor sermon URL verified this session.
